items.fix: Treat coverage as a *map* — use it to find gaps, not as a score to maximise.
items.why: Gaming the metric (weak asserts, trivial calls) raises the number without raising quality.
items.example: Aim for 'every interesting branch tested', not '100%'.

items.mistake: Using only line coverage on non-trivial logic.
items.fix: Always pass `--cov-branch` for code with conditionals.
items.why: Line coverage misses untaken branches — 100% line can be 50% branch.

items.mistake: Setting a single global coverage target.
items.fix: Set per-module thresholds: 90%+ on critical modules, 60-70% on CLI/scripts.
items.why: A flat target either under-tests critical modules or over-tests trivial ones.
items.example: Use `fail_under` per package or use `diff-cover` for diff-only enforcement.

items.mistake: Excluding files/lines from coverage without explaining why.
items.fix: Every `# pragma: no cover` and every `omit` entry gets a comment explaining the reason.
items.why: Otherwise the exclusion becomes folklore — code that should be tested silently isn't.
items.example:
```python
# pragma: no cover  -- exercised only in nightly smoke suite
```

items.fix: Pair high unit-test coverage with integration tests that exercise real dependencies.
items.why: Coverage measures *your* code execution; mocked dependencies might not match reality.
items.example: 100% coverage with mocked LLM ≠ working LLM integration. Add integration / smoke tests.
